coredata.dc:title: Ractopamine as a novel reagent for the fabrication of gold nanoparticles: Colorimetric sensing of cysteine and Hg2+ ion with different spectral characteristics
coredata.dc:creator: Kateshiya, Mehul R.
George, Gincy
Rohit, Jigneshkumar V.
Malek, Naved I.
Kumar Kailasa, Suresh
coredata.dc:description: We have developed a facile and cost-effective chemical reduction procedure for the preparation of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) using ractopamine hydrochloride (RA) as a novel reagent. The RA assembled AuNPs display high affinity towards cysteine (Cys) and Hg2+ ion, causing aggregation of RA-AuNPs, which accompanies a remarkable a red-shift (534 to 606 nm) for Cys and a blue-shift (534 to 506 nm) for Hg2+ ion in the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band of RA-AuNPs. The UV–visible absorption spectral data revealed that linear calibration curves were obtained between absorption ratios (A606/A534 for Cys and A506 for Hg2+ ion) and the concentration range from 0.6 to 600 µM and from 1 to 400 µM with linear coefficients of 0.9910 and 0.9925 for Cys and Hg2+ ion. The RA-AuNPs-based colorimetric assay represents the detection limits of 0.35 and 0.30 µM for Cys and Hg2+ ion (3σ). This colorimetry assay is completed within 60 min, and successfully applied to detect Cys in urine and Hg2+ ion in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: RA-AuNPs
Cys
Hg2+ ion
UV–visible spectroscopy
TEM and real samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of AuNPs using ractopamine (RA) as a reducing agent
Colorimetric sensing ability of RA-AuNPs towards cysteine and Hg2+ ion
Analysis of cysteine in urine and Hg2+ ion in water samples using RA-AuNPs as a probe
Colorimetric assay of cysteine and Hg2+ ion and sensing mechanisms
Effect of pH and NaCl concentration
Practical application of RA-AuNPs for assaying of Cys in urine and Hg2+ ion in water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
Department of Science and Technology, Government of Kerala

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under Fast-Track Young Scientist Scheme (No.SR/FT/CS-54/2010). MK acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India for the doctoral fellowship to carry this work.

This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under Fast-Track Young Scientist Scheme (No.SR/FT/CS-54/2010). MK acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India for the doctoral fellowship to carry this work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effect of RA concentrations (50 to 200 mM) for the preparation of AuNPs with controlled SPR peak using 50 mM of HAuCl4 solution.
TEM image of synthesized RA-AuNPs at (a) 50 nm and (b) 10 nm scale bars. Inset: the corresponding size distribution of RA-AuNPs in water.

Investigation of RA-AuNPs as a novel amino acid colorimetric sensor via absorption spectral changes in RA-AuNPs with various amino acids (alanine, aspartic acid, glutamic acid, phenylalanine, threonine, leucine, valine, glutamine, cysteine, glycine, isoleucine, methionine, serine, histidine hydrochloride, tryptophan, asparagine, proline, arginine hydrochloride, hydroxyproline, lysine, tyrosine. 1.0 mM). Inset: photographic image of RA-AuNPs with amino acids.

Investigation of RA-AuNPs as a novel metal ion sensor via absorption spectral changes in RA-AuNPs with various metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Cr3+, 1.0 mM). Inset: photographic image of RA-AuNPs with metal ions.

Development of analytical method by measuring absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs with addition of different concentrations of (a) Cys (0.6–800 µM) at 8 pH in the presence of 50 µL (5 mM) NaCl and of (b) Hg2+ ion (1.0–500 µM) without any buffer pH at 50 µL (5 mM) NaCl. Insets: Images of respective RA-AuNPs solutions with Cys and Hg2+ ion.
RA as a novel ligand for fabrication of AuNPs and colorimetric applications for recognition of Cys and Hg2+ ion.
Comparison of the present method for the detection of Cys with the reported methods in the literature.
Comparison of analytical performance of the present method for the detection of Hg2+ ion with the reported methods.

head.ce:title: Ractopamine as a novel reagent for the fabrication of gold nanoparticles: Colorimetric sensing of cysteine and Hg2+ ion with different spectral characteristics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat 395007, Gujarat, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Srinagar, Hazratbal Kashmir 190006, Kashmir, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Srinagar, Hazratbal Kashmir 190006, Kashmir, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have developed a facile and cost-effective chemical reduction procedure for the preparation of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) using ractopamine hydrochloride (RA) as a novel reagent. The RA assembled AuNPs display high affinity towards cysteine (Cys) and Hg2+ ion, causing aggregation of RA-AuNPs, which accompanies a remarkable a red-shift (534 to 606 nm) for Cys and a blue-shift (534 to 506 nm) for Hg2+ ion in the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band of RA-AuNPs. The UV–visible absorption spectral data revealed that linear calibration curves were obtained between absorption ratios (A606/A534 for Cys and A506 for Hg2+ ion) and the concentration range from 0.6 to 600 µM and from 1 to 400 µM with linear coefficients of 0.9910 and 0.9925 for Cys and Hg2+ ion. The RA-AuNPs-based colorimetric assay represents the detection limits of 0.35 and 0.30 µM for Cys and Hg2+ ion (3σ). This colorimetry assay is completed within 60 min, and successfully applied to detect Cys in urine and Hg2+ ion in water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Over past few decades, a great revolution has been occurred on the use of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as ideal visual probes for colorimetric assay of chemical and biochemical species with specificity and sensitivity [1–3]. Since AuNPs exhibit unique behavior in visible range due to SPR phenomenon [1]. Importantly, the colorimetric recognition ability has greatly dependent on the surface chemistry of AuNPs [3]. In survey of literature revealed that various organic molecules i.e., 2,3,4-trihydroxy benzophenone [4], dicoumarol [5], tyrosine [6–7], quercetin [8], 4-amino antipyrine [9], 4-amino nicotinic acid [10], pyrogallol acid [11], ampicillin [12], tyrosine [13], gallic acid [14–15], N-acetylglucosamine [16], histidine [17], alkanethiol [18], cyclodextrins [19] and diaminodiphenyl sulfone [20] have been successfully incorporated as promising ligands for the synthesis of AuNPs with SPR band around 510 – 530 nm. The above ligands were provided effective and unique molecular architectures on the AuNPs surfaces, allowing them to act as ideal agents for colorimetric recognition of ultra-trace level molecules with high selectivity.

Cysteine (Cys) is well known as mercapto amino acid that widely found in biofluid (human plasma) [21]. It is one of the key molecule for protein synthesis, biochemical pathways in various cells and detoxification [22]. It has been noticed that the total concentration of Cys in healthy persons is in the range of 25–200 µM [23]. Noticeably, excess levels of Cys may lead to the formation of stone in kidney and ureters, causing cystinuria disease [21–23], which reveals that the levels of Cys in biofluids could be considered as a marker for the identification of cystinuria disease. Thus, various nanomaterials including AgNPs-hydroxyapatite [24], AuNPs-Rhodamine B [25], polymer decorated AuNPs [26], cysteamine/EDTA-AuNPs [27], bagasse extract-AgNPs [28], tree bark extract-AuNPs [29], pectinase-AuNPs [30], Zonyl FSN-100-AuNPs [31], carbon dots-AuNPs [32], N-acetyl-L-cysteine-AuNPs [33], platinum cages [34] and functionalized AuNPs [35] have been integrated with UV–visible and fluorescence spectrometric techniques for assay of Cys in biofluids.

Mercuric ion (Hg2+ ion), a severe toxic pollutant that readily accumulates into the body via food chain [36]. It inhibits the biological pathways of various key organs, causing severe damage to central nervous system, liver and brain [37]. Thus, assay of Hg2+ ion is of great importance in environmental monitoring system. Recently, various metal NPs including AuNPs have been integrated with spectroscopic techniques for the analysis of Hg2+ion in various ecological systems. Briefly, 2-mercaptobenzothiazole-AuNPs [38], NaYF4:Yb3+, Tm3+ NPs [38], poly-L-lysine (PLL)- cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)-AgNPs [40], cysteamine-nanodimond [41], citrate-AuNPs [42], and 11-mecaptoundecanoic acid (MUA)-AuNPs [43], carbon dots [44], gold nanoclusters [45] and BODIPY-based colorimetric fluorescent sensor [46] have been used as recognition agents for the analysis of Hg2+ ion in various samples.

Here, a new organic molecule namely ractopamine hydrochloride (RA) is introduced as a potential ligand for the preparation of AuNPs with controlled size and SPR band. The electron microscopic and spectroscopic analysis demonstrated that RA molecules provide unique molecular assembly on AuNPs surfaces, which helpful to segregate AuNPs with controlled size and absorption properties. The RA-AuNPs provide a simple way to establish UV–visible spectrometry as an ideal and potential analytical tool for parallel detection of cysteine (Cys) via red-sfhit and Hg2+ ion via blue-shift (Scheme 1 ). The as-fabricated RA-AuNPs showed good stability in water and exhibited different surface plasmon resonance (SPR) changes and significant color changes from red to blue. Using this method, the detection limits for Cys and Hg2+ ion are estimated to be 0.35 and 0.30 µM, respectively. The RA-AuNPs-based colorimetric approach has shown ability to quantify Cys and Hg2+ ion concentrations in real samples.
Chemicals (HAuCl4·xH2O, metal salts and amino acids) were procured from Sigma-Aldrich, USA and SRL Pvt. Ltd., India. Ractopamine hydrochloride was received as a gift sample from Thinq Pharma, India.

The spectral characteristics and morphology of RA-AuNPs were examined by using spectroscopic (a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer, Ocean Optics, USA; Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (FT-IR spectrum BX, Perkin Elmer, Germany) and transmission electron microscopic (TEM) (Tecnai 20, Philips, Holland) techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AuNPs using ractopamine (RA) as a reducing agent
ce:section.ce:para: In this, RA was introduced as a novel ligand for the fabrication of aqueous dispersed AuNPs. Briefly, the solution of gold salt (50.0 mM) was prepared by dissolving HAuCl4·3H2O in deionized water. The solution was boiled for 5 min under constant stirring. Then, 2.0 mL of 150 mM RA was added into the above solution to induce AuNPs growth, and the solution was boiled under the same conditions for 180 min. The color of the solution was turned into dark red, demonstrating the formation of RA-AuNPs. In order to examine the effect of RA concentration, AuNPs were synthesized using different concentration of RA from 50 to 200 mM under the above identical conditions. The RA-AuNPs were kept at 4 °C for further characterization and their applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric sensing ability of RA-AuNPs towards cysteine and Hg2+ ion
ce:section.ce:para: Analytical applications of RA-AuNPs were evaluated by investigating the absorption spectral changes of RA-AuNPs in the presence of biomolecules and metal ions. Briefly, 0.5 mL of Cys (0.6–800 µM) was introduced into 1.0 mL of RA-AuNPs solution and vials were vortexed for 10 min. The changes in the color were measured by digital camera and the solutions corresponding absorption spectra were recorded on Ocean Optics spectrophotometer. The absorption ratio at A606/A534 was used to estimate the concentration of Cys and the selectivity of RA-AuNPs towards Cys was evaluated in the presence of other biomolecules and inorganic species.

For Hg2+ ion detection: 1.0 mL of RA-AuNPs was taken into a series of vials and then, different concentrations of Hg2+ ion (1.0 – 500 µM) transferred into the above solutions. The vials were incubated for 15 min and their spectral changes were measured by Ocean Optics Spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of cysteine in urine and Hg2+ ion in water samples using RA-AuNPs as a probe
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical use of the method, human urine was collected from healthy volunteers at Applied Chemistry Department, S.V. National Institute of Technology, Surat, India. The collected urine (0.1 mL) samples were diluted with 0.4 mL of deionized water to minimize sample matrix during the analyte assay. Various concentrations of Cys (10, 50 and 100 µM) were spiked into the above urine samples and then RA-AuNPs solution was added. The amount of Cys was estimated by the above procedure (Table S1 of Supporting Information). Similarly, the developed probe was applied to detect Hg2+ ion water samples (Tap, River and Canal water). The water samples were fortified with Hg2+ ion (10, 50 and 100 µM) and the amount of Hg2+ ion was estimated by using calibration graph via standard addition method and the results were tabulated in Table S2 of Supporting Information.

In order to generate AuNPs with best spectral characteristics, the effect of RA concentration was investigated on the fabrication of RA-AuNPs. In this, HAuCl4/RA molar ratio (HAuCl4:RA, 50:50, 50:100, 50:150 and 50:200, mM:mM) was selected for the synthesis of RA-AuNPs and the corresponding absorption spectra were depicted in Fig. 1 . Based on the appearance of surface plasmon resonance (SPR) peak intensity at 534 nm, HAuCl4/RA (50:150, mM:mM) molar ratio was selected as the best reagent concentration for the reduction of Au3+ ion to AuNPs with effective RA molecular assembly. The RA-AuNPs showed the maximum SPR intensity without any broaden spectra, suggesting the AuNPs are formed with uniform size ~ 20 nm. The schematic synthetic route for RA-AuNPs synthesis is shown in Scheme 1. The UV–visible absorption, FT-IR spectra and TEM image of RA-AuNPs were shown in Figs. 1, 2 and Fig. S1 of Supporting Information. The absorption spectrum of RA-AuNPs displayed a SPR peak at 534 nm, showing red color in day light, which confirms that the formation of RA-AuNPs. To confirm the size and morphology of RA-AuNPs, TEM was used for the characterization of RA-AuNPs (Fig. 2). The TEM micrographs revealed that RA-AuNPs are good dispersity and spherical shape with an average size of 8.5 ± 2.5 quasi-spherical (Fig. 2), demonstrating the as-synthesized RA-AuNPs are dispersed in water without any agglomeration. The FT-IR spectrum of unreacted RA shows the presence of phenolic groups at 3334 cm−1 and secondary amine group stretching at 3120 cm−1. In FT-IR spectrum of RA-AuNPs, the appearance of characteristics phenolic groups was slightly shifted due to the involvement of Au3+ ion reduction to Au0. The oxidized RA molecules were assembled on the surfaces of AuNPs. FT-IR spectral data confirmed that the RA-AuNPs contain –OH and –NH groups. The stability of RA-AuNPs was investigated by measuring the absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs in everyday (1 to 7 days). The SPR intensity of RA-AuNPs did not show any drastic changes up to 6 days, however the SPR intensity of RA-AuNPs was greatly reduced and spectrum was broadened (Fig. S2 of Supporting Information). These studies revealed that the stability of synthesized RA-AuNPs could enough to apply RA-AuNPs as a colorimetric probe for the detection of Hg2+ and Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric assay of cysteine and Hg2+ ion and sensing mechanisms
ce:section.ce:para: The possible sensing mechanism for Cys and Hg2+ ion detection, the ractopamine contains electron-donating groups (–OH and –NH). The oxidized RA molecules were self-assembled on the surfaces of AuNPs. Among 21 aminoacids, Cys has strongly induced the color and spectral changes of RA-AuNPs. Since mercapto group of Cys has strong ability to adsorb onto the surfaces of RA-AuNPs, favoring to form covalent bond between Cys-RA-AuNPs, which results a remarkable change in the color and absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs. As a result, the SPR absorption band of RA-AuNPs (534 nm) was shifted to longer wavelength (606 nm) due to the electric dipole–dipole interaction, thereby favoring to decrease interparticle distances of RA-AuNPs. Furthermore, free thiol group of Cys is a very good binding affinity with the surfaces of AuNPs [47] as compared to other aminoacids, thereby inducing the aggregation of RA-AuNPs, which results a color change from red to purple. Several AuNPs-based colorimetric methods have been described for the detection of target analytes including Hg2+ ion [38–40,42–43] and all the methods are based on a remarkable red-shift in the SPR band of AuNPs due to the color change from red to blue via AuNPs aggregations. Interestingly, a blue-shift was observed in the SPR band of RA-AuNPs with addition of Hg2+ ion and the color of RA-AuNPs-Hg2+ ion solution was remained constant. A significant blue shift was noticed in the SPR band of RA-AuNPs from 534 to 506 nm with increasing concentration of Hg2+ ion. Since Hg2+ ion could adsorb onto the surface of RA-AuNPs, resulting to form Hg-AuNPs core–shell nanostructures via metallophilic interactions that avoids the aggregation of RA-AuNPs. Furthermore, Hg2+ ion has strong affinity towards AuNPs to form core–shell nanostructures via d10-d10 interactions between Hg2+ ion and AuNPs [45,48], which yields to generate Au-Hg amalgam. This results a drastic blue-shift in the SPR band of RA-AuNPs without any color change, which could be used for the detection of Hg2+ ion by measuring the SPR band intensities of RA-AuNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and NaCl concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The RA-AuNPs aggregations were evaluated by measuring the SPR absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs with Cys and Hg2+ ion at various buffer pHs i.e., sodium acetate, sodium citrate and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) from 2.0 to 12.0 in the absence and presence of both Cys and Hg2+ ion (Figs. S3-S6 of Supporting Information). The visual aggregation and SPR absorption spectral changes of RA-AuNPs were not occurred in the absence of Cys at PBS pH 2.0 – 12.0, however there are slight visual aggregations and spectral changes at acidic pH due to the self-aggregation of RA-AuNPs. Clearly, there are remarkable visual aggregations and SPR absorption spectral changes in RA-AuNPs with Cys at PBS pH 2–12. The intense visual aggregation and the maximum SPR absorption ratio at A606/A534 were occurred at PBS pH 8, revealing the PBS pH 8 is the optimum pH for colorimetric assay of Cys using RA-AuNPs as a probe. Since the positive charge of Cys can decrease with increasing pH, thereby also reducing the electrostatic repulsions between RA-AuNPs and Cys, which is due to isoelectric point of Cys (pI = 5.02) [49]. The Cys-RA-AuNPs aggregation rate is high at pH 8.0 because of the formation of covalent bond between SH group of Cys and AuNPs. It is also noticed that the degree of Cys-AuNPs aggregation could be increased with increasing buffer pH salts due to the shielding effect [35,49]. Therefore, PBS pH 8 was selected as optimum pH for sensing of Cys using RA-AuNPs as a probe. Noticeably, it was observed that the visual aggregation and significant SPR absorption spectral change were maximum in the presence of Hg2+ ion without PBS pH (Fig. S6 of Supporting Information). Further, the detection of Hg2+ ion is based on the increase in the intensity of RA-AuNPs at 506 nm. Thus, the assays of Cys (at PBS pH 8) and Hg2+ ion (without PBS) are based on the red-shift for Cys and blue-shift Hg2+ ion in the SPR band of RA-AuNPs.

Furthermore, the ionic strength of solution has shown great impact on the formation of analyte-AuNPs aggregates [35]. It was noticed that the AuNPs aggregation was noticed in the presence of NaCl without analytes due to the shielding effect of NaCl [49]. The addition of NaCl into AuNPs solution can cause the aggregation of AuNPs due to the minimization of interparticle distances between neighboring AuNPs via reduced electrostatic repulsions between AuNPs. The analytical performance of AuNPs was greatly improved by using optimum concentration of NaCl as an ionic strength [35]. Thus, the effect of NaCl concentration on the SPR absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs was investigated. In this, 1.0 mL of RA-AuNPs was treated with different concentrations (5– 20 mM) of NaCl (Fig. S7 of Supporting Information). With increasing concentration of NaCl, the SPR absorption spectra are slightly broaden and the SPR peak intensity is decreased. However, 5.0 mM of NaCl did not show any remarkable change on the absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs. Thus, 5.0 mM of NaCl was selected as an optimum concentration for assaying of Cys and Hg2+ ion using RA-AuNPs as a probe.

According to the observation of absorption spectra, Cys and Hg2+ ion concentrations were estimated by measuring the absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs with introduction of different amounts of Cys and Hg2+ ion individually. The influence of different amounts of Cys (0.6–800 µM) and Hg2+ ion (1–500 µM) on the absorption spectral characteristics of RA-AuNPs are shown in Fig. 5 a-b. For Cys, the SPR band of RA-AuNPs was shown bathochromic shift (red-shift) in the absorption spectra and the intensity of red-shift is proportional to the concentration of Cys. The intensity of SPR band of RA-AuNPs at 606 nm progressively increases, demonstrating the formation of Cys-RA-AuNPs aggregations. The color of RA-AuNPs changes from red to blue as a function of Cys, revealing the development of visual assay for Cys with RA-AuNPs (Inset of Fig. 5a). By plotting Cys concentration (0.6–600 µM) against A606/A534, showing a linear regression (y = 0.0582x + 0.5901, where × is the concentration of Cys) with correlation coefficient of 0.9910 (S/N = 3) (Fig. S8a of Supporting Information). In the case of Hg2+ ion, the absorbance value of RA-AuNPs is progressively increased from 534 to 506 nm, confirming the blue-shift in the SPR band of RA-AuNPs with the addition of Hg2+ ion. The absorption intensity of RA-AuNPs at 506 nm is increased linearly with increasing Hg2+ ion concentration (1.0–500 µM). A straight line linear regression (y = 0.1105x + 0.9666, x is Hg2+ ion concentration) with a correlation coefficient of 0.9925 was obtained between the intensity of SPR peak and concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 1.0 – 400 µM (Fig. S8b of Supporting Information). The detection limits (LOD = 3σ/s) are estimated to be 0.35 and 0.30 µM for Cys and Hg2+ ion, where they can comparable with the reported analytical methods. The analytical features (linear range and LOD) of present method for the detection of Cys and Hg2+ ion are depicted in Tables 1-2
. Table 1 demonstrates that the LOD of present method for Cys is lower than that of reported method [24] and comparable with AuNPs-based approach [29] in the literature. Similarly, the present method also shows lower LOD as compared to the reported methods in the literature [40,44] (Table 2). Even though the LOD of present method for Hg2+ ion in drinking water did not meet the guidelines of European Union (the maximum level for Hg2+ in drinking is 5 nM) and U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (the maximum level of Hg2+ is 10 nM), but the LOD of the present method for Hg2+ ion is lower than the maximum permissible limit of Hg2+ ion (1.34 to 0.59 µM) in industrial effluents recommended by Environmental Pollution Board, India. Therefore, the present method could be used for the detection of Cys in biofluids and of Hg2+ ion in industrial effluents at minimal volume of samples.

To explore the selectivity of RA-AuNPs for Cys and Hg2+ ion, we recorded the UV/vis absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs by the introduction of various interfering metal ions, anions, biomolecules and pesticides along with and without target analytes (Cys and Hg2+). The UV/vis absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs exhibited a remarkable change only in the presence of Cys (red-shift) and of Hg2+ ion (blue-shift), whereas other interfering species did not cause any significant change in the absorption spectral characteristics of RA-AuNPs (Figs. S9-S10 of Supporting Information). Further, the red RA-AuNPs solution was turned into blue for Cys and faded red color for Hg2+ ion, whereas no obvious color change was noticed with other chemical species. These data revealed that RA-AuNPs displayed good selectivity towards Cys and Hg2+ ion in the presence other interfering chemical species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of RA-AuNPs for assaying of Cys in urine and Hg2+ ion in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the practical application of RA-AuNPs for Cys and Hg2+ ion, urine and natural water samples (tap, river and canal waters, Surat, Gujarat, India) were taken and monitored by using RA-AuNPs as a sensor. Under optimal conditions, 0.1 mL of urine was mixed with 0.4 mL of water and then added 1.0 mL of RA-AuNPs. The absorption spectral changes of the above solutions were measured. The amounts of Cys and Hg2+ ion were estimated by the standard addition procedure (Table S1-S2 of Supporting Information). The recovery ranges of the method are in the range of 97.66–101.99% for Cys in urine and of 99.26–101.20% for Hg2+ ion in water samples with relative standard deviation < 0.9%. These analytical data demonstrated that the practical application of RA-AuNPs for assaying of Cys and Hg2+ ion with good accuracy and precision.

In this work, synthesis of RA-AuNPs was carried out by a facile and single step reaction, which exhibits the good sensitive to colorimetric recognition of Cys and Hg2+ ion. The colorimetric assay is based on the red-shift (534 to 606 nm) for Cys and blue-shift (534 to 506 nm) for Hg2+ ion in the absorption spectra of RA-AuNPs, revealing the both analytes showed different spectral characteristics. This method exhibited straight line linearity in the ranges of (0.6–600 µM) and (1.0–400 µM) for Cys and Hg2+ ion with LODs of 0.35 and 0.30 µM for Cys and Hg2+ ion. The developed probe provides a simple analytical platform for colorimetric recognition of Cys and Hg2+ ion with different spectral characteristics. Further, the analytical application of as-synthesized RA-AuNPs was extended for the analysis of Cys in urine and of Hg2+ ion in water samples, demonstrating the RA-AuNPs-based Cys and Hg2+ ion colorimetric probe is rapid and trouble-free portable analytical approach for assaying of Cys and Hg2+ ion in real samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India under Fast-Track Young Scientist Scheme (No.SR/FT/CS-54/2010). MK acknowledges the Director, SVNIT, Surat, India for the doctoral fellowship to carry this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105212.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
